introduction:
The scriptures are full of amazing stories -- prophets, kings, battles, miracles, and covenants. But those stories happened in a real place, to real people, during real historical periods. When you know the history behind the scriptures, the stories come alive.

This book walks you through the history of Israel from the time Joshua led the twelve tribes into the promised land all the way to the destruction of the temple in 70 AD. That's roughly 1,400 years of history. You'll learn about the judges, the kings, the prophets, the exile, the rebuilding, the Greeks, the Maccabees, and the Romans. You'll see how the world Jesus was born into got to be the way it was.

This isn't a replacement for scripture study -- it's a companion to it. When you read about Elijah on Mount Carmel, you'll know what was happening in the Northern Kingdom and why it mattered. When you read about the Savior driving the money changers from the temple, you'll understand what that temple looked like and who controlled it. When you read about Lehi leaving Jerusalem, you'll know exactly what he was leaving behind -- and what was about to happen to the city he left.

Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes. Read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few questions in your own words. There's no rush -- the goal is real understanding, not speed.
description: A narrative history of Israel from the conquest of Canaan through the destruction of the Second Temple in 70 AD, written for Latter-day Saint kids. Covers the judges, kings, prophets, exile, return, Greek and Roman periods, and the world Jesus was born into -- giving young readers the historical context that makes scripture study richer.
what_youll_learn: Why the promised land mattered and what it looked like
How Israel went from tribes to a kingdom -- and then split in two
What the prophets were really doing and why people didn't listen
How the Northern Kingdom fell to Assyria and the ten tribes were lost
Why Lehi left Jerusalem and what happened to the city after he left
How the Jews rebuilt after the Babylonian exile
What happened during the Greek and Maccabean periods
How Rome took over and what the world looked like when Jesus was born
Why the temple was destroyed in 70 AD and what it meant for Israel
expectations: 15 chapters, designed for one chapter per week (4 short lessons per week). Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes -- read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few short questions in your own words. Your work is graded by the tutor and you can take a chapter as many times as you need.
prerequisites: None. No prior scripture knowledge is assumed, though the book will feel most relevant to kids with some exposure to Bible and Book of Mormon stories.
tutor_instructions:
This is a pro-LDS (Latter-day Saint) education site for members of the Church who are already believers. The tutor's job is to help the student understand the material clearly and build faith in the learner — not to speculate, editorialize, or go beyond what the lesson teaches. Stay on the topic of the current lesson. Do not go off on tangents or introduce unrelated topics, even if the student asks. Gently redirect them back to the lesson material.

This book is a narrative history of Israel written for Latter-day Saint kids ages 7-12. The reader may have heard many of these stories in Primary or family scripture study but likely doesn't have a strong sense of the timeline or historical context.

When explaining and when grading:
- Use plain, simple words. Prefer short sentences. Avoid academic or theological jargon.
- Examples should connect to things kids already know from church, Primary, Come Follow Me, or family scripture study.
- LDS perspective is the default. Treat the scriptural record as historical. Reference the Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price naturally where relevant.
- Do not be apologetic or evangelical -- these kids are already members. Be matter-of-fact.
- Help kids connect the historical timeline to scriptures they may already know. 'Remember when you learned about Lehi leaving Jerusalem? That happened during the same time as this lesson.'
- When grading, accept any answer that captures the lesson's core idea in the student's own words. Don't require exact dates or names unless the question specifically asks for them.
- Be encouraging. The goal is building a mental map of Israel's history so scripture study is richer.
